buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

prices = pd.read_csv('prices.csv', parse_dates=['timestamp'])
news = pd.read_csv('news_sentiment.csv', parse_dates=['timestamp'])

# merge_asof with no tolerance — matches any past news, even years old
merged = pd.merge_asof(
    prices.sort_values('timestamp'),
    news.sort_values('timestamp'),
    on='timestamp',
    direction='backward'
)

# Rows with no recent news use sentiment from potentially very old articles
merged['sentiment'] = merged['sentiment_score'].fillna(0)
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

prices = pd.read_csv('prices.csv', parse_dates=['timestamp'])
news = pd.read_csv('news_sentiment.csv', parse_dates=['timestamp'])

# merge_asof with 1-day tolerance — only use recent news
merged = pd.merge_asof(
    prices.sort_values('timestamp'),
    news.sort_values('timestamp'),
    on='timestamp',
    direction='backward',
    tolerance=pd.Timedelta('1D')
)

# Rows with no recent news get NaN — acknowledged as missing, not stale
merged['sentiment'] = merged['sentiment_score'].fillna(0)
description: merge_asof without tolerance matches events from arbitrarily far in the past.
explanation: Without a tolerance parameter, merge_asof will match the most recent news article even if it was published 2 years ago. A price row in 2023 could be matched to news from 2021. That sentiment signal is completely stale and irrelevant, but the code treats it as valid current data.
suggested_fix: Add a tolerance parameter to limit how far back to look for matching events: tolerance=pd.Timedelta('1D')

issues.title: merge_asof without tolerance uses arbitrarily stale event data
merge_asof without tolerance uses arbitrarily stale event data
